title: ॐ जय जगदीश हरे आरती
devanagariText:
॥ श्री जगदीश जी की आरती ॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे, स्वामी जय जगदीश हरे।
भक्त जनों के संकट, दास जनों के संकट,
क्षण में दूर करे॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

जो ध्यावे फल पावे, दुःख बिनसे मन का।
स्वामी दुःख बिनसे मन का।
सुख सम्पत्ति घर आवे, सुख सम्पत्ति घर आवे,
कष्ट मिटे तन का॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

मात पिता तुम मेरे, शरण गहूँ मैं किसकी।
स्वामी शरण गहूँ मैं किसकी।
तुम बिन और न दूजा, तुम बिन और न दूजा,
आस करूँ मैं जिसकी॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

तुम पूरण परमात्मा, तुम अन्तर्यामी।
स्वामी तुम अन्तर्यामी।
पारब्रह्म परमेश्वर, पारब्रह्म परमेश्वर,
तुम सब के स्वामी॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

तुम करुणा के सागर, तुम पालनकर्ता।
स्वामी तुम पालनकर्ता।
मैं मूरख खल कामी, मैं सेवक तुम स्वामी,
कृपा करो भर्ता॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

तुम हो एक अगोचर, सबके प्राणपति।
स्वामी सबके प्राणपति।
किस विध मिलूँ दयामय, किस विध मिलूँ दयामय,
तुमको मैं कुमति॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

दीनबन्धु दुःखहर्ता, ठाकुर तुम मेरे।
स्वामी ठाकुर तुम मेरे।
अपने हाथ उठाओ, अपनी शरण लगाओ,
द्वार पड़ा तेरे॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

विषय विकार मिटाओ, पाप हरो देवा।
स्वामी पाप हरो देवा।
श्रद्धा भक्ति बढ़ाओ, श्रद्धा भक्ति बढ़ाओ,
सन्तन की सेवा॥

ॐ जय जगदीश हरे॥

॥ इति श्री जगदीश जी की आरती सम्पूर्ण ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Jagdish Ji Ki Aarti ||

Om Jai Jagdish Hare, Swami Jai Jagdish Hare.
Bhakt Janon Ke Sankat, Das Janon Ke Sankat,
Kshan Mein Door Kare.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Jo Dhyave Phal Pave, Dukh Binase Man Ka.
Swami Dukh Binase Man Ka.
Sukh Sampatti Ghar Aave, Sukh Sampatti Ghar Aave,
Kasht Mite Tan Ka.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Maat Pita Tum Mere, Sharan Gahun Main Kiski.
Swami Sharan Gahun Main Kiski.
Tum Bin Aur Na Dooja, Tum Bin Aur Na Dooja,
Aas Karun Main Jiski.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Tum Puran Parmatma, Tum Antaryami.
Swami Tum Antaryami.
Parbrahm Parmeshwar, Parbrahm Parmeshwar,
Tum Sab Ke Swami.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Tum Karuna Ke Sagar, Tum Palankarta.
Swami Tum Palankarta.
Main Murakh Khal Kami, Main Sevak Tum Swami,
Kripa Karo Bharta.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Tum Ho Ek Agochar, Sabke Pranpati.
Swami Sabke Pranpati.
Kis Vidh Milun Dayamay, Kis Vidh Milun Dayamay,
Tumko Main Kumati.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Deenbandhu Dukhharta, Thakur Tum Mere.
Swami Thakur Tum Mere.
Apne Hath Uthaao, Apni Sharan Lagaao,
Dwar Pada Tere.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

Vishay Vikar Mitaao, Paap Haro Deva.
Swami Paap Haro Deva.
Shraddha Bhakti Badhaao, Shraddha Bhakti Badhaao,
Santan Ki Seva.

Om Jai Jagdish Hare.

|| Iti Shri Jagdish Ji Ki Aarti Sampurna ||
hindiMeaning:
ॐ जय जगदीश हरे! हे स्वामी! भक्तजनों और दासों के संकट क्षण में दूर करने वाले!

जो तुम्हारा ध्यान करता है वह फल पाता है, मन का दुःख नष्ट होता है। घर में सुख-सम्पत्ति आती है, तन का कष्ट मिटता है।

तुम मेरे माता-पिता हो, मैं किसकी शरण लूँ? तुम्हारे बिना कोई दूसरा नहीं जिसकी आशा करूँ।

तुम पूर्ण परमात्मा हो, तुम अन्तर्यामी हो। परब्रह्म परमेश्वर हो, तुम सबके स्वामी हो।

तुम करुणा के सागर हो, तुम पालनकर्ता हो। मैं मूर्ख, दुष्ट और कामी हूँ; मैं सेवक हूँ, तुम स्वामी हो — कृपा करो भर्ता!

तुम एक अगोचर हो, सबके प्राणपति हो। हे दयामय! मैं कुमति हूँ, किस विधि से तुमसे मिलूँ?

हे दीनबन्धु! दुःखहर्ता! ठाकुर तुम मेरे हो। अपने हाथ उठाओ, अपनी शरण में लगाओ — तुम्हारे द्वार पड़ा हूँ।

विषय-विकार मिटाओ, पाप हरो हे देवा! श्रद्धा-भक्ति बढ़ाओ, सन्तों की सेवा में लगाओ।
benefits: THE most popular universal aarti in Hinduism - sung in virtually every Hindu household
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of sorrows and obstacles of devotees instantly
Traditionally associated with prosperity, happiness, and peace to the home
Can be sung for any deity - universally applicable
Traditionally recited for removal of all sins and increases faith and devotion
Composed by Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri in 1870 - beloved for over 150 years
Ideal for evening aarti and all puja occasions
description: Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, the Om Jai Jagdish Hare (ॐ जय जगदीश हरे आरती) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. Drawn from Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri, this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Regular recitation is believed to bring prosperity, happiness, and peace to the home, remove all sorrows and obstacles of devotees instantly, and composed by Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri in 1870 - beloved for over 150 years. This prayer is traditionally recited on Thursday (Guruvar) and Sunday (Ravivar) to invoke divine grace. Comprising 8 verses, it can be completed in approximately 5 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Om Jai Jagdish Hare in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Om Jai Jagdish Hare Aarti in Hindi with meaning. The most popular universal Hindu aarti for daily worship, evening prayers and all puja occasions.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Om Jai Jagdish Hare aarti?
seoFaqs.answer: Om Jai Jagdish Hare (ॐ जय जगदीश हरे) is the most popular and universally recited aarti in Hinduism — a six-stanza Hindi-Brajbhasha devotional song composed by Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri of Phillaur (Punjab) in 1870. Although addressed to Bhagavan Vishnu as Jagdish Hare ('Lord of the Universe, Hari'), the aarti is sung as the concluding aarti of every household and temple puja across India regardless of the deity worshipped — Vishnu, Krishna, Rama, Lakshmi, Durga, Ganesh, Shiva or any ishta-devata — because its language is universal and addressed to the supreme Lord of all creation. With its memorable melody and simple Hindi vocabulary, it has been the single most universally recognised Hindu prayer for over 150 years. (Source: Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri, 1870; classical Sanatana Dharma aarti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri, the composer of the aarti?
seoFaqs.answer: Pandit Shardha Ram Phillauri (1837-1881) was a Sanatana Dharma scholar, novelist, social reformer and spiritual orator born in Phillaur, Jalandhar district, Punjab. He is renowned as the composer of Om Jai Jagdish Hare (1870), which he originally created as the conclusion to a katha-pravachan he was delivering on the Bhagavat Purana. He is also recognised as the author of the first Punjabi-language novel Bhagyavati (1877), which made him a pioneer of modern Punjabi literature. As a tireless preacher of Sanatana Dharma during a period of intense missionary activity in Punjab, he toured villages defending Hindu customs and devotional practice. The aarti spread rapidly from his discourses across northern India and within decades became the universal aarti of Hindu households. He passed away at the young age of 44, leaving behind one of the most beloved spiritual compositions of modern Hinduism. (Source: classical Sanatana Dharma history; Punjab cultural history.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the opening line Om Jai Jagdish Hare?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening refrain of the aarti — Om Jai Jagdish Hare, Swami Jai Jagdish Hare; Bhakt janon ke sankat, Das janon ke sankat, Kshan mein door kare — translates as: 'Om! Victory to You, Jagdish Hare (Lord of the Universe, Hari / Vishnu)! Victory to You, my Swami (Lord and Master)! In a single moment You remove the troubles (sankat) of devotees, the troubles of all who serve You.' The word Jagdish (जगदीश = Jagat + Isha) means 'Lord of the Universe', and Hare is the vocative of Hari, one of the principal names of Vishnu meaning 'the Remover' (of sins, sufferings, cycles of birth and death). The repeated refrain establishes both the universal sovereignty of God and His instant compassion towards every devotee — making this the perfect concluding prayer to any worship. (Source: Om Jai Jagdish Hare aarti, opening verse.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how is the Om Jai Jagdish Hare aarti sung?
seoFaqs.answer: The Om Jai Jagdish Hare aarti is the universal concluding aarti sung at: (1) the morning and evening daily mandir aarti in virtually every Hindu household across India and the diaspora, (2) the conclusion of every Satyanarayan vrat-katha — for which it has become inseparable, (3) Diwali Lakshmi puja — the climactic aarti sung after the Lakshmi-Ganesh puja, (4) Janmashtami midnight aarti, (5) Ram Navami, (6) Hanuman Jayanti, (7) every grih-pravesha (housewarming) and vivaha (wedding) ceremony, (8) every annaprashan, mundan, upanayana and naming-ceremony samskara, and (9) every Hindu festival regardless of deity. The aarti is sung while circling the brass-plate aarti diya (with a wick of camphor or ghee) in clockwise circles before the deity, with the entire family standing and clapping rhythmically. After the aarti, devotees pass their hands over the flame and touch their eyes to receive the divine blessing. (Source: Sanatana Dharma household worship tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Om Jai Jagdish Hare considered universal — applicable to any deity?
seoFaqs.answer: Om Jai Jagdish Hare is regarded as a universal aarti because its language is generic enough to apply to any form of God yet personal enough to feel addressed to one's own ishta-devata. The names used — Jagdish (Lord of the Universe), Hare (Hari), Swami (Master), Parmatma (Supreme Self), Antaryami (Indweller), Parbrahm (Supreme Brahman), Parmeshwar (Supreme God), Karuna ke Sagar (Ocean of Compassion), Palankarta (Sustainer), Pranpati (Lord of Life), Deenbandhu (Friend of the Lowly), Dukhharta (Remover of Sorrow), Thakur (Lord) — are all generic divine names that fit any deity. Additionally, regional traditions have created Krishna-themed (Om Jai Shiv Omkara for Shiva, Jai Ambe Gauri for Durga) and other deity-specific versions on the same melody, but the original Om Jai Jagdish Hare remains the most beloved. This universality is why it is the one aarti every Hindu, regardless of sampradaya, knows by heart. (Source: classical Sanatana Dharma household tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does daily recitation of Om Jai Jagdish Hare grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition and pious household practice attribute the following blessings to daily recitation of the Om Jai Jagdish Hare aarti: (1) instant removal of sankat (troubles, calamities, obstacles) — as promised in the very opening verse 'Bhakt janon ke sankat... Kshan mein door kare', (2) sukh-sampatti (happiness and prosperity) entering the home — verse 2: 'Sukh sampatti ghar aave, Kasht mite tan ka', (3) protection by the Lord as one's mother, father, brother, friend, knowledge and wealth — verse 3, (4) recognition that God is the Antaryami (indweller of all hearts) and Parbrahm Parmeshwar — verse 4, (5) cultivation of the disciple's bhava that one is a humble servant and the Lord is the merciful master — verse 5, (6) destruction of vishaya-vikar (sense-attachments and mental distortions) and papa (sins) — verse 8, and (7) increase of shraddha-bhakti (faith and devotion) and the impulse to serve the saints (santan ki seva) — verse 8. Above all, it brings the warmth of family worship and the felt presence of God in every Hindu home. (Source: Om Jai Jagdish Hare aarti internal verses.)
